segments.text: So this time I'm going to be talking about transformers,
kind of the backbone of most implementations,
not only in natural language processing,
but also a wide variety of other things as well.
And I'm going to be talking both about transformers
as they were originally conceived and implemented in 2017.
And also some modifications that people
makes to transformers today to make them work much better
in kind of modern language models such as Lama.
So I'll talk about both of those at the same time.
So as a Quimper Dinder, I just want to review the attention
from last time very quickly.
And basically, if you remember attention,
there were two varieties of attention.
One was cross-attention, where you
attend to another sentence, basically, or another sequence.
So you have one sequence that serves as your keys
that you are attending to, and one sequence that
serves as your queries, the things that you are using
to attend to the sequence of keys.
And so you can do that for every element in the query vector
attending to every element in the key vector.
And the other alternative is self-attention,
where you are attending to the same sequence.
So basically, you're guaranteed that the queries
and the keys attend, like correspond to the same sequence.
And so that's really the only difference
between self-attentioning cross-attention.
Transformer-based models use either a self-attention
or they use self-attention and cross-attention.
So I'll talk a little bit about two different types
of transformer-based models that use both of those.
And the way we calculated the tension
was basically by using the query vectors,
taking all the key vectors.

you keep between them like this.

to make sure they all add up to one in our B2-0.
